# Acyclic Dependencies

**Flexibility: Suggested (MAY)**

## The Principle

Engineering teams MAY organize their systems so that dependency graphs —
at the package level, the service level, and the repository level —
remain acyclic. When a cycle is detected, teams MAY invest in breaking it
through interface extraction, dependency inversion, or architectural
restructuring.

## Concept Foundation

- **JOS-C25 (Abstraction Leakage)** — Cyclic dependencies are a
  structural form of abstraction leakage. When A depends on B and B
  depends on A, the boundary between them is fictional — they are one
  unit pretending to be two. This pretense leaks through every
  abstraction layer: you cannot deploy A without B, test A without B,
  or reason about A without understanding B.
- **JOS-C24 (Cognitive Load Budgeting)** — Cycles multiply the cognitive
  load required to understand a system. A developer tracing a call path
  through a cyclic graph enters an infinite loop of context. Acyclic
  graphs have a natural topological order that allows incremental
  comprehension from leaf to root.

## Why This Matters

Dependency cycles are the root cause of many engineering pain points:
builds that must compile everything together, deployments that must
ship multiple services atomically, test suites that cannot run in
isolation, and refactoring efforts that cascade unpredictably. Breaking
cycles unlocks independent development, independent deployment, and
independent reasoning. Each of these independently reduces coordination
cost; together they transform team velocity.

## Tension and Tradeoffs

Some cycles are pragmatic and intentional. A bidirectional protocol
between two services may create a logical cycle that is simpler than the
alternatives. The "suggested" flexibility level acknowledges this — teams
are encouraged to prefer acyclic structures but are not mandated to
eliminate every cycle at any cost. The key is awareness: teams should know
where their cycles are, why they exist, and what it would cost to break
them. Undetected cycles are the dangerous ones.

## 1970/2070 Note

In 1970, cyclic dependencies manifested as mutual COMMON blocks in
Fortran or circular includes in C. In 2070, they may appear as circular
agent-to-agent invocations or recursive AI pipeline dependencies. The
structural pathology is identical regardless of the technology: cycles
create coupling that resists decomposition.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain principle extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer principles.
